Atypical Cell Cycle Regulation over Neural Stem Cell Expansion

Ingrid  Qemo*,  Dorota  Lubanska*,  Keith  Franklin  Stringer,  Bre-Anne  Fifield,  Youshaa Elabed, Hema  Priya Mahendran,  Sami  Alrashed,  Emmanuel  Boujeke, Alexander Rodzinka, Alexandra Sorge, Srinath Kandalam, Jillian Brown, Hasan Ghafoor, Maheen Arshad, Lisa A. Porter £

*Contributed equally

£ Corresponding author:

University of Windsor Ontario Windsor, ON N9B 3P4 Phone: (519) 253-3000 x4775 Fax: (519) 571-3609 lporter@uwindsor.ca

SUMMARY

Populations  of  quiescent  adult  neural  stem  cells  (NSCs)  that  reside  in  the  mammalian brain  aid  in  neurogenesis  throughout  life  and  can  be  identified  by  molecular  markers including Nestin, a type VI intermediate filament protein. Cell cycle regulation plays an important  role  in  determining  the  fate  of  NSCs  in  the  adult  brain  and  maintaining  a crucial balance between self-renewal and differentiation. Data from our group and others support  that  the  atypical  cyclin-like  protein  Spy1  (also  called  RingoA;  gene SPDYA ) plays a critical role in activating NSCs from a quiescent state. Elevated levels of Spy1 are found in  aggressive  human  brain  cancers,  including  glioblastoma.  Using  a  conditional mouse model, we demonstrate that driving the expression of Spy1 in the Nestin-enriched NSC  population  of  the  brain  directs  the  cells  toward  symmetric  cellular  division, increases stemness characteristics, decreases differentiation, and increases susceptibility to oncogenic transformation &  drug resistance. This study contributes to better understanding  of  intricate  cell  cycle  mechanisms  which  lead  to  deviation  from  the homeostatic state, promoting aberrant changes in adult NSCs.

INTRODUCTION

Neurogenesis  is  the  process  by  which  new  neurons  and  glial  cells  are  formed  in  the brain.  This  phenomenon  occurs  primarily  during  embryonic  development,  but  it  also persists  to  a  limited  extent  in  the  adult  brain  in  select  neurogenic  niches  including  the lateral ventricles of the subventricular zone (SVZ), and dentate gyrus of the subgranular zone  (SGZ) 1, 2 .  Neurogenesis  dictates  normal  brain  development,  brain  plasticity, cognitive function, memory, emotional regulation, and regenerative capacity of the brain. Abnormal neurogenesis can manifest across a spectrum of developmental, neurological, and  psychiatric  outcomes  and  may  support  susceptibility  to  neurodegenerative  and/or neuroproliferative diseases and syndromes including carcinogenesis.

Long-lived neural stem cells (NSC) possess the remarkable ability to self-renew and  differentiate  into  all  of  the  functional  cell  types  within  the  brain.  Molecular biomarkers  have  been  used  in  attempt  to  unambiguously  categorize  NSCs  and  their progeny in the adult SVZ. Nestin is one marker of NSCs and transient amplifying cell populations (progenitor cells) of the SVZ 3 .    Data  suggests that Nestin is necessary for NSC self-renewal and survival 4 . Nestin-positive cells are exclusively found in regions of the  brain  that  harbor  a  niche  tailored  to  the  maintenance  of  stem  and  progenitor populations 5 where NSCs are maintained in a state of quiescence 6 .

Mechanistically, NSCs respond to environmental and extracellular stimuli, growth factors, cytokines, and cues from the niche and surrounding microenvironment to trigger neurogenesis.  During  embryonic  development  NSCs  are  in  a  constant  state  of  selfrenewal  and  differentiation  with  a  cell  cycle  length  of  only  ~8  hours,  however  adult NSCs increase the length of the cell cycle dramatically with up to a 4-fold increase in G1

phase  of  the  cell  cycle,  and  as  adults  assume  a  state  of  quiescence  to  preserve  their population 7, 8. Triggering of neurogenesis in adult NSCs requires exiting quiescence and re-entering  the  cell  cycle 9 .  The  tumor  suppressor  p53  is  upregulated  in  the  quiescent neurogenic niches of the brain where it maintains an equilibrium between self-renewal and  differentiation  regulating  activity  of  the  G1/S  Cyclin  Dependent  Kinase  (CDK), CDK2  10 .    Inactivation  of  p53  results  in  an  activation  of  quiescent  stem  cells  and  an expansion of NSCs 11, 12, 13 .   Loss/inactivation of p53 is found in many human tumours and  is  attributed  to  boosting  the  stemness  properties  of  cancer  cells  contributing  to aggressive, drug resistant disease 14, 15, 16 .

Spy1 protein (gene SPDYA ; also called RingoA)  directly binds to, and activates, both  CDK1  and  CDK2  in  a  unique fashion that is independent of activating phosphorylation and dephosphorylation events 15, 16 . As a result of this mechanism Spy1 has  been  isolated  from  numerous  screens  and  has  been  described  for  the  ability  to override cell cycle arrest mechanisms including DNA damage and cellular senescence 19, 20 . Data from our lab show that Spy1 can enhance pluripotent reprograming by epigenetic downregulation  of  CDK  inhibitors  and  is  essential  for  overcoming  reprogramminginduced senescence 21 . Work from the Nebreda lab has recently demonstrated that Spy1 is required  for  activation  of  NSCs  out  of  quiescent  state  via  activation  of  CDK2 22 .  The authors showed that brains of mice deficient in Spy1 have decreased neurogenesis and accumulate  quiescent  cell  populations 22 .  Our  lab  has  also  shown  that  in  mammary progenitors, Spy1 plays an important role during normal development, and Spy1 protein levels accumulate to inhibit differentiation 23 . Spy1 levels elevate following acute sciatic nerve  injury,  suggesting  a  potential  role  in  CNS  regeneration 24 .  Endogenous  levels  of Spy1  are  elevated  in  several  types  of  cancer 25, 26, 27 and  Spy1  regulates  symmetric division  and  expansion  of  aggressive  populations  of  brain  tumour  initiating  cells  in human  glioma 28 .  Mechanistically,  the  Spy1-CDK  complex  overrides  the  inhibitory effects  of  downstream  p53  targets,  p27  and  p21 20 and  work  from  our  lab  has demonstrated that p53 targets Spy1 for degradation in breast and immortalized cell lines 29 .  Hence,  the  literature  supports  that  p53  holds  the  Spy1  protein  in  check  in  select systems.  Whether  Spy1  alone  can  override  the  protective  effects  of  p53  in  NSCs  and drive phenotypes resembling a brain-targeted p53 null mouse is an interesting question that we sought to address.

Using  a  conditional  mouse  model  this  work  characterises  the  consequence  of elevated Spy1 protein levels in Nestin-positive populations in the mammalian brain. We show that Spy1 delays neuronal differentiation, promotes symmetric expansion of NSCs and increases susceptibility to oncogenic transformation in  vitro, mimicking the neural phenotypes seen with loss of p53 30 . We further provide data to support that select drivers of glioma rely on endogenous Spy1 to maintain tumour progression in vivo. This work contributes to better understanding of the cell cycle mechanisms capable of tipping the balance  of  decisions  in  NSCs  capable  of  predisposing  this  population  of  cells  toward supporting oncogenesis.

RESULTS

Construction and generation of pTRE-Spy1 transgenic mouse

To address the role of Spy1 in NSC populations of the mammalian brain, we generated a transgenic mouse system, termed NTA-Spy1, which permits inducible, spatially homogenous  expression  of  Spy1  throughout  the  course  of  development  as  originally described by the Chodosh group 31 .  This mouse model has uniquely allowed the control of  Spy1  under  the  Nestin  promoter.  The  Flag-Spy1  sequence  was  removed  from  a previously described vector - Flag-Spy1A-pLXSN and was cloned into a pTRE-TightCaspase3 vector backbone (Figure S1A). A restriction digest using XhoI and AlwNI was performed to remove the 536bp portion of the plasmid that was used for microinjection (Figure  S1B).  PCR  analysis  depicted  pTRE-Spy1  positive  founder  mice  through  the presence of a 536bp band (Figure S1C). The pTRE-Spy1 mice express the open reading frame  of  Spy1  downstream  of  a  promoter  containing  a  series  of  tetracycline  operator sequences. Lastly, the positive founder mice of a B6CBAF1/J background were found to transmit the pTRE-Spy1 transgene to their progeny (Figure S1D).

Generation and characterization of the NTA-Spy1 transgenic mouse

To create the NTA-Spy1 mouse, pTRE-Spy1 transgenic mice were crossed with NestinrtTA  mice  (Figure  1A).  This  ensured  that  Spy1  would  be  expressed  under  the  Nestin promoter in the presence of doxycycline. Nestin is an intermediate filament protein that begins to accumulate in the neural precursor cells of the brain at embryonic day 12 (E12) and is retained in NSC populations throughout adult life 32 . NSCs comprise a very small population of the brain and are in unique areas, one of which is the SVZ. Post-natal (PN) day 2 brains were micro-dissected to remove the SVZ. Extracted cells from the SVZ were cultured in the presence or absence of doxycycline and qRT-PCR was performed for Flag and Spy1 mRNA expression in the NTA-Spy1 and all other littermate controls. NTASpy1  mice  show  significant  increase  in  Flag  and  Spy1  expression  in  the  presence  of doxycycline as compared to littermate controls (Figure 1B). Primary cells harvested from the SVZ were also used for western blot analysis. NTA-Spy1 doxycycline-treated NSCs showed an increase in Spy1 protein when compared to NTA-Spy1 cells not treated with doxycycline as  well  as  pTRE-Spy1  littermate  controls  with  or  without  doxycycline.  A representative  western  blot  (left)  has  been  shown  and  protein  levels  were  measured  in relative  units  for  densitometry  values  (right)  (Figure  1C).  To  determine  if  Spy1  levels were being upregulated specifically in the Nestin positive population, mouse SVZ were dissected and subjected to fluorescence associated cell sorting for Nestin. The positive and  negative  Nestin  cell  populations  from  both  the  NTA-Spy1  and  littermate  control pTRE-Spy1 mice were analyzed through qRT-PCR for Spy1 levels (Figure 1D). There was  a  significant  increase  in  Spy1  levels  within  each  genotype  when  comparing  the Nestin positive and negative populations. There was also a significant increase in Spy1 levels between the Nestin positive populations between NTA-Spy1 and pTRE-Spy1.

Enhanced proliferation and NSC expansion in NTA-Spy1 mice

Transient overexpression of Spy1 in wildtype NSCs extracted from the SVZ reveals an increase in gene expression levels of key stem cell markers Musashi 1 (Msi1), CD133, Bmi1,  Vimentin  and  Nestin  as  compared  to  empty  vector  control  (Figure  S2).  This supports a role for Spy1 levels in regulating stemness properties of NSCs. To assess the consequence of elevated Spy1 levels in the NSC population of the NTA-Spy1 mice, we performed SVZ cell extractions from NTA-Spy1 mouse brains along with their littermate controls.  NTA-Spy1 derived NSCs presented elevated levels of proliferation in culture in comparison to all controls as assessed by a BrdU incorporation assay (Figure 2A). The percent  of  BrdU  positive  cells  was  calculated  as  BrdU+  cells  over  the  total  cell population (right). NTA-Spy1 cells had a significantly higher percentage of BrdU+ cells compared to all other control cells. Neurosphere formation assays correlate with in vivo self-renewal and proliferation of NSCs 33, 34 . A neurosphere formation assay (Figure 2B) revealed  that  NTA-Spy1  cells  form  significantly  more  spheres  over  serial  passages (Figure  2B;  left),  and  that  neurospheres  are  generally  larger  in  size  (Figure  2C)  as compared to controls, suggesting enhanced stemness and proliferation among NTA-Spy1 derived NSCs.

A  limitation  of  the  neurosphere  formation  assay  is  that  it  does  not  have  the capacity  to  discriminate  between  spheres  that  were  derived  from  single  cells  versus spheres that have formed due to an aggregation of cells. The clonality self-renewal assay, in  which  neurospheres  are  dissociated  and  seeded  as  single  cells  in  96-well  low attachment  plates,  circumvents  this  limitation.  When  we  performed  a  single  cell  selfrenewal assay, we showed that NTA-Spy1 cells had a higher proportion of self-renewing NSCs  (Figure  2D).  NTA-Spy1  also  had  a  significant  increase  in  gene  expression  for stemness markers CD133, Sox2 and Nestin throughout serial passaging (Figure 2E). In addition, densitometric analysis of protein expression revealed that Sox2 protein levels, were  significantly  elevated  in  the  NTA-Spy1  NSC  population  as  compared  to  pTRESpy1 cells (Figure S3A).

Interestingly,  upon  further  investigation  of  reprogramming  transcription  factor genes Oct4, Sox2, c-Myc, Klf4 and Nanog 35, 36 , qRT-PCR analysis revealed that NTASpy1  cells  have  upregulated  levels  of  reprogramming  gene  expression  as  compared  to pTRE-Spy1 littermate controls (Figure 2F). We then tested the gene expression levels of defined  reprogramming transcription  factors  essential  for  propagating  tumour  initiating cells from differentiated progeny 37 .  We found increases of all stemness factors in cells derived from NTA-Spy1 mouse in comparison to pTRE-Spy1 control (Figure S3B). Due to an intrinsic increase in reprogramming genes, we wanted to determine whether NTASpy1  cells  had  the  potential  to  sustain  self-renewal  regardless  of  being  cultured  in differentiation  promoting  media.  We  employed  a  re-sphering  assay,  as  previously described 38 .  We  dissociated neurospheres that had grown in EGF and FGF-containing media  and  plated  single  cells  at  400  cells  per  well  on  96-well  plates  in  2%  FBS containing  media  to  promote  differentiation  (Figure  S3C).  Initially,  we  observed  cell differentiation  and  there  was  no  visible  sphere  formation,  however,  new  neurospheres started appearing in the differentiation conditions 2 weeks post-differentiation. A higher percentage  of  cells  generated  spheres  in  the  NTA-Spy1  cohort  as  compared  to  pTRESpy1 controls (Figure S3D; left). The re-sphered cells remained attached in 2D during the span  of  the  differentiation  experiment  (Figure  S3D;  right).  qRT-PCR  analysis  of differentiation markers S-100 β (astrocyte marker), Foxo4 (O4) (oligodendrocyte marker), and Mapt (neuronal marker) show an increase during differentiation compared to initial neurospheres and decreased in the newly formed spheres compared to differentiated cells (Figure  S3E).  Interestingly,  NTA-Spy1  cells  had  significantly  lower  expression  of  all differentiation  markers  throughout  all  stages  relative  to  the  pTRE-Spy1  control.  This suggests that the cells that were capable of re-sphering were primed for this prior to being subjected to  differentiation.  We  were  able  to  remove  and dissociate  individual  spheres and plate them back into neurosphere culture where they were able to continue expanding as 3D floating neurospheres.

NTA-Spy1 mice exhibit decreased neural differentiation capacity

NSCs that are taken out of EGF and FGF-containing neurosphere cultures in the presence of FBS will start to differentiate. To determine the effect of increased levels of Spy1 on NSC lineage differentiation, we cultured NSCs in adherent conditions in the presence of 2% FBS without growth factors to induce differentiation.  Morphology was used to score differentiated cells at the indicated time points. Cells that contained outgrowth processes of at least two cell-body diameters in length were scored as differentiated. We found that NTA-Spy1  cells  had  a  significant  decrease  in  differentiation  potential  compared  to control littermates, as can be seen in representative images (right) and scored percentage of differentiated cells (left) (Figure 3A). To distinguish if there was a neural differentiation lineage that may  have  been  impaired  more  than  others,  we  used differentiation protocols to drive the NSCs down astrocyte, oligodendrocyte, or neuronal lineages. NSCs  were  plated on polyornithine coated dishes and given different supplements  in  their  respective  mediums.  Gene  expression  of  S-100 β, O4,  Beta  III tubulin (neuronal marker) as well as Mapt was analyzed via qRT-PCR at 0, 48 and 96 hours of the differentiation time course (Figure 3B). Although NTA-Spy1 cells show a decrease in differentiation capacity to astrocyte and oligodendrocyte lineages, the biggest and most significant  effect  was  seen  in the  neuronal  lineage.  NTA-Spy1  cells  have  an early decrease in neuronal differentiation capacity compared to control pTRE-Spy1 cells. The CDK inhibitor p27 enhances neural differentiation and we have previously shown that  Spy1-CDK  actively  targets  p27  for  degradation 39,  40,  41, 42 .  Hence,  prior  to  the commencement of differentiation, protein expression of p27 was analyzed in pTRE-Spy1 and NTA-Spy1 cells through western blotting. We found that there was significantly less p27  protein  in  NTA-Spy1  compared  to  pTRE-Spy1  controls  (Figure  3C).    These experiments  demonstrate  that  Spy1-mediated  effects  on  neuronal  differentiation  occur concomitant  with  an  activation  of  cell  cycle  progression  and  a  reduction  in  p27 degradation.

NTA-Spy1 NSC shift towards Musashi 1-mediated symmetric mode of division

We have previously demonstrated that Spy1 drives expansion of human glioma stem cells through  regulating  their  mode  of  division 28 .  Asymmetric  cell  division  supports  the terminal differentiation identity of neurons in C. elegans 44 . In Drosophila and mice, the basic molecular mechanism  of  asymmetric  cell division involves the subcellular localization of cell fate determining protein Numb 45,  46,  47, 48 .  To  assess the impacts of NTA-Spy1 on cell fate in the presence of signals from the extracellular matrix, pTRESpy1  and  NTA-Spy1  cells  were  cultured  at  low  densities  in  Matrigel.  Elevated  Spy1 levels resulted in over 3-fold increase in the number of 3D colonies that were formed and a significant decrease in the percentage of differentiated cells (Figure 4A). To determine any changes in mode of division in the NTA-Spy1 mice, we performed a clonal assay in MaxGel ECM and monitored the distribution of Numb between mitotic cell pairs. NTASpy1 cells presented with increased symmetric distribution of Numb protein (Figure 4B; left), indicative of an increase in symmetric division. Analysis of mitotic cell pairs with symmetric  and  asymmetric  Numb  distribution  over  the  total  cell  pairs  was  performed (Figure 4B; right) and verified the significant increase in symmetric cell division and a decrease  in  asymmetric  division  in  NTA-Spy1  cell  as  compared  to  control  littermate pTRE-Spy1.

The Notch pathway plays a vital role in NSC maintenance and self-renewal 49-51 (Figure  4C).  Notch  expression  is  regulated  by  Numb  and  Musashi  l  (Msi1) 52 .  Msi1 activation  and  function  is  directly  induced  through  phosphorylation  by  Spy1-bound CDK2  53 .  Western blot analysis (Figure 4D; left) revealed that Msi1 protein levels are increased in NTA-Spy1 cells as compared to pTRE-Spy1 (Figure 4D). This corresponded to  significantly  decreased  levels  of  Numb  protein  (Figure  4D).  To  test  whether  Msi1 plays  a  role  in  Spy1-mediated  symmetry  of  division,  we  used  short  interference  RNA (siRNA)  against  Msi1.  qRT-PCR  analysis  confirmed  a  significant  decrease  in  Msi1 mRNA  levels  and  a decrease in Notch  transcriptional targets, Hes1 and Hes5, downstream of Msi1 (Figure 4E). We performed a clonal assay to determine whether the increased  symmetric  division  seen  in  NTA-Spy1  cells  was  mediated through  the  Msi1 pathway. When we use siMsi1 we find that there is a decrease in symmetric cell division in  NTA-Spy1  cells  bringing  levels  to  that  which  is  comparable  to  pTRE-Spy1  control cells (Figure 4F). This suggests that Spy1 is increasing symmetric cell division in NTASpy1 cells through the Msi1 signaling pathway.

Aging NTA- Spy1 mice retain NSCs and present impaired cognitive functions

To characterize the capacity of NTA-Spy1 mice to maintain the pools of NSCs we tested the Nestin+ population of cells in aging NTA-Spy1 mice. The neurogenic regions of the brain  were  dissected  from  20-month-old  mice.  Cells  derived  from  NTA-Spy1  mice showed  not  only  significantly  increased  proliferation  in  comparison  to  controls  as assessed by BrdU incorporation assay (Figure 5A) but continued to demonstrate a block in differentiation at  each  time  point tested  in aging primary Nestin+ cells (Figure 5B). Staining  of  the  brain  sections  obtained  from  20-month-old  mice  with  PCNA  revealed upregulation of the number of PCNA+ cells in NTA-Spy1 mice in comparison to control mice (Figure 5C).  In  addition,  NTA-Spy1  cells  showed  increased  mRNA  levels  of  the proliferation  marker  Ki67  (Figure  5D),  as  well  as  decreased  levels  of  CKI  gene expression of p21 ( Cdkn1a ), INK4 ( Cdkn2a ), and p53 ( Trp53 ) (Figure 5E) as compared to  pTRE-Spy1  cells.  Histone γ H2A.X  is  the  most  common  marker  for  DNA  double strand breaks and telomere shortening 54,  55 ,  and at times can also be used as a cellular senescence  marker 56 .  Notably,  when  we  performed  immunofluorescence  for γ H2A.X foci  we  found  that  NTA-Spy1  cells  had  a  decrease  in  the  number  of γ H2A.X  foci compared to control littermates  (Figure  5F).  We  also  found a  decrease  in  apoptosis as measured  by  Caspase  3/7  activity  in  the  NTA-Spy1  cells  compared  to  pTRE-Spy1 controls (Figure 5G). This suggests that Nestin+ cells of NTA-Spy1 aging mice continue to proliferate with no signs of NSC pool depletion by any means.

To  determine  how  the  observed  blockage  in  differentiation  affects  neuronal layering  along  the  cortex,  we  stained  sections  with  a  neuron  specific  stain,  Nissl,  and scored  Nissl+  cells.  NTA-Spy1  mice  showed  a  shift  in  neuronal  positioning  of  the cortical  plate  (Figure  5H)  with  obvious  accumulation  of  neuronal  bodies  in  closer proximity to ventricular zone as compared to Nestin-rtTA control mice, suggesting the role of Spy1 in controlling differentiation timing during development. Finally, we sought to  establish  if  the  observed  changes  to  neuronal  positioning  were  affecting  cognitive functions in mice. We performed the novel object recognition task, as demonstrated by the schematic (Figure 5I; top). Interestingly, NTA-Spy1 mice had significantly decreased preference  for  novel  objects  brought  to  the  task  set  up  as  compared  to  their  littermate controls (Figure 5I; bottom).  In summary, upregulation of Spy1 levels in Nestin+ cells results in abnormal positioning of neurons and impairs cognitive functions in NTA-Spy1 mice.

Spy1 increases proliferation of transformed NSCs Given the established role of Spy1 in several types of cancer including malignancies of neural origin, we were interested in investigating whether the elevated levels of Spy1 in the  NTA  mice  would  contribute  to  transformation  of  NSCs  in  face  of  applied  prooncogenic changes. We first studied the effects of Spy1 overexpression on the expression pattern of selected tumour suppressor genes in the mouse brain through immunostaining of the brain sections of NTA-Spy1 mice versus control with antibodies against p53 and PTEN. We found upregulated expression of p53 in the cells of the hippocampus of NTASpy1 mice, while no difference was observed in cells of the subventricular zone or cortex (Figure  6A).  Our  results  revealed  a  significant  increase  of  Pten  expression  in  the hippocampus  and  cortex  of  NTA-Spy1  mice  compared  to  control,  with  no  difference observed in the subventricular zone (Figure 6B). These findings suggest that NTA-Spy1 mice exhibit molecular changes associated with a response to counteract potential prooncogenic  effects  of  Spy1.  To  further  explore  the  importance  of  Spy1  in  potentially aberrant changes in NSC populations, we downregulated the levels of p53 and inhibited Pten using shRNA and bpV(HOpic), PTEN inhibitor (PTENi), respectively, in primary cultures  from  NTA-Spy1  and  control  pTRE-Spy1  mice  (Figure  S4A  &  B).  Gene expression analysis revealed that there was an overall significant increase in Cyclin D1 and  c-Myc  levels  in  the  NTA-Spy1  in  comparison  to  the  pTRE-Spy1  cultures  (Figure 6C). The expression levels of selected targets were further elevated with downregulation of p53 and inhibition of Pten individually and in combination in NTA-Spy1 derived cells compared to the pTRE-Spy1 control (Figure 6C). This data supports that overexpression of Spy1 protein levels correlate with alterations in other genes/proteins associated with increasing proliferation and stemness found in brain malignancies.

Spy1  enhances  oncogenic  transformation  in  NSCs  in  the  presence  of  selected aberrant events

A hallmark characteristic of oncogenic transformation is the ability to survive and proliferate  in  low  attachment  conditions  (Figure  S5A) 57 .  Cultures  derived  from  NTASpy1  or  pTRE-Spy1  mice  were  transduced  with  shp53,  RasV12,  using  lentivirus,  and treated with PTENi or vehicle control in the presence and/or absence of doxycycline and subjected to low attachment conditions using ultra-low attachment plates for five days. Viability  was  assessed  using  3D  Cell  Titer  Glo  assay  and  revealed  that  pTRE-Spy1 littermate controls had significantly higher viability when grown in attachment conditions, consistent with the growth preference of non-transformed cells (Figure S5B, top).  However,  NTA-Spy1  cells  exhibited  similar  viability  in  both  growth  conditions (Figure  S5B, bottom),  suggesting  enhanced  survival capacity  of  NTA-Spy1  cultures in non-adherent conditions as compared to littermate controls. The viability of NTA-Spy1 cultures is  significantly  enhanced in ultra-low attachment conditions in the presence of overexpression of RasV12 and/or combined with the treatment with PTENi and siRNA against p53 (Figure S5D), this was not seen with inhibition/knockdown of PTEN or p53 alone  (Figure  S5C).  This  supports  the  hypothesis  that  NTA-Spy1  cells  are  more susceptible  to  oncogenic  transformation.  To  further  explore  this  result,  we  conducted classical soft agar colony formation assay in NTA-Spy1 cells compared to pTRE-Spy1 controls  with  combined  attenuation  of  p53  and  PTEN.  At  21  days  post-seeding, transformation potential of the cells was assessed by scoring the colonies with a diameter equal  to  or  greater  than  50  microns.  A  significantly  higher  number  of  colonies  were generated  from  NTA-Spy1  cells  in  comparison  to  cells  from  the  pTRE-Spy1  control

(Figure 7A). There was also a significant increase in the number of colonies in the NTASpy1 in comparison to pTRE-Spy1 upon p53 knockdown and PTENi treatment, alone. Significant increase in the number of colonies was observed in NTA-Spy1 NSCs which had  both  the  shp53  and  PTENi  combined  (Figure  7A).  We  then  conducted  a  colony formation assay using NTA-Spy1 and pTRE-Spy1 cells transduced with RasV12 and/or control.  Although the overexpression of Ras resulted in colonies forming regardless  of Spy1  overexpression,  the  NTA-Spy1  NSCs  generated  significantly  more  colonies  that pTRE-Spy1  cells  (Figure  7B).  The  significant  increase  in  transformation  potential  of NTA-Spy1 cells compared to pTRE-Spy1 control are consistent with the suggestion that elevated Spy1 levels facilitate the path toward oncogenic transformation. To address the capacity  of  NTA-Spy1  to  support  tumour  formation,  we  made  use  of  a  zebrafish xenograft  model.  NTA-Spy1  cells  or  littermate  pTRE-Spy1  controls,  with  or  without sip53,  were  engrafted  into  zebrafish  larvae  at  48  hours  post  fertilization  (hpf)  and  the number of resulting foci per larvae was scored 4 days after injection (Figure 7C, left). There was a significant increase in foci formation in sip53 NTA-Spy1 cells over sip53 pTRE-Spy1 control (Figure 7C, right), notably sip53 also increased foci in all conditions over  control  as  one  would  expect.  To  investigate  the  importance  of  Spy1  in  brain tumourigenesis in a  murine  model,  GL261  mouse  glioma  cells  driven  by  p53  and Ras mutations, were transduced with shRNA against Spy1 or a scrambled control. The cells were transplanted orthotopically into neonatal C57BL/6 mice of syngeneic background. 14  days  post  injection  7/8  mice  injected  with  the  control  developed  visible  tumours whereas  tumours  from  shSpy1  cells  were  found  in  4/8  mice  at  the  14-day  timepoint (Figure  7D,  left).  Analysis  of  the  tumour  burden  at  15  days  post  injection  showed significantly downregulated brain tumour size in mice injected with shSpy1 cells when compared to the scrambled control (Figure 7D, right). The data shows that Spy1 not only cooperates  with  known  aberrant  changes  found  in  glioma  to  induce  tumourigenesis  in normal  NSCs  but  also  contributes  to  proliferation/growth  in  tumours  driven  by  the selected mutations.

It has previously been shown that Spy1 overrides the DNA damage response in a variety  of  other  cell  types 58, 59, 60, 29 .  To  determine  whether  NTA-Spy1  NSCs  have altered response to DNA  damage  cells  were  treated with the standard of care chemotherapy  relied  upon  in  the  treatment  of  many  neural  tumours,  temozolomide (TMZ).  NTA-Spy1  cells  demonstrated  significantly lower levels of apoptosis as measured  by  Caspase  3/7  activity  assay  in  comparison  to  Nestin-rtTA  control  cells (Figure  7E,  left).  The  treatment  resistance  was  further  enhanced  in  NTA-Spy1  NSCs when p53 knockdown was introduced (Figure 7E, right). Overexpression of RasV12 in the  NTA-Spy1  cells  further  increased  resistance  to  cell  cycle  arrest  mediated  by  a synthetic CKI, NU2058, and/or TMZ, alone and in combination (Figure 7F).

DISCUSSION

In normal brain it is accepted that proliferative cell cycle genes support the expansion of the NSC pool while limiting successful neurogenesis 61, 20 . Much less understood is how NSCs balance cell cycle states such as quiescence to maintain homeostasis. A study by Gonzalez et al . demonstrated the essentiality of the atypical cell cycle regulator Spy1 in the activation of NSC pools from the quiescent state followed by decreased neurogenesis 22 , the molecular mechanisms by which Spy1 regulates the NSC pool in the normal adult brain  remain  fundamentally  unknown.  In  human  CD133+  brain  tumour  initiating  cells derived from glioma patients, symmetric division regulated by Spy1 is critical for clonal expansion of this cell population 28 . This study established the NTA-Spy1 mouse model which  permits  spatial  control  of  the  expression  of  Spy1  under  the  Nestin  promoter allowing for detailed study of Spy1  mediated  cell cycle effects throughout the development  of  CNS  and  in  response  to  potential  malignant  changes  in  the  pools  of selected NSCs.

Our in  vitro data  supports  that  increased  levels  of  Spy1  in  NSCs  prevent functional differentiation down  all lineages and enhance stemness capacity.  We demonstrate that Spy1 increases proliferation while maintaining stem cell attributes of the Nestin+  population  in  the  brains  of  both  young  and  aging  mice.  We  also  show  that increased  levels  of  Spy1  in  the  NSCs  of  these  mice  exhibit  impaired  functional differentiation  induced  by  the  removal  of  the  growth  factors  EGF  and  FGF,  especially down  the  neuronal  lineage.  Our  results  suggest  that  the  inhibition  of  neurogenesis  in NTA-Spy1 mice is two-fold: 1) a decrease in the differentiation programme, and 2) an increase in symmetric cell division, both of which are holding NSCs in a stem cell state, regardless of cues. Furthermore, we see a subtle impairment in recognition memory due to an inhibition of differentiation. Our work establishes a role for Spy1 in inducing a shift in cell fate decisions that favour symmetric division over asymmetric division in NSCs and  thereby  maintain  prolonged  self-renewal,  in  the  aging  brain.  A  shift  towards  a proliferative  symmetric  mode  of  division is thought  to  occur due  to  a  reduction in  the length of the G1 phase, which ultimately inhibits neurogenesis and has been reported to contribute to oncogenic transformation in multiple models before 62, 63, 64 .

A well-established regulatory mechanism in mode of division is the segregation of  the  cell  fate  determinant protein Numb which is negatively regulated via activity of Msi1. Moreover, Numb inhibits the Notch signaling pathway, which is critical to stem cell self-renewal and symmetric division 65 . Spy1/CDK signalling has been implicated as a  conserved  mechanism  for  activation  of  Msi1  in  mammalian  cells  and  during  oocyte maturation 53 .  Our results show that Spy1 regulates mode of division in NSCs via Msi1 and NTA-Spy1 cells downregulate classical transcriptional targets of Notch1 pathway in the face of Msi1 knockdown.  Hence, Spy1/CDK2- mediated effects upstream of Msi1 may potentially  be  involved  in  memory.  It  is  important  to  stress,  however,  that  at  this time we cannot draw explicit details with regards to how increased Msi1 through Spy1 changes the neural populations of cells that are involved with memory. Further work will aid  in  elucidating  specific molecular  mechanisms that could be altered in neurogenesis that also play important roles in memory as well as potentially memory-related disorders.

Msi1-Numb-Notch  axis  has  been also implicated previously in malignant transformation 66 and  our  work  provides  evidence  that  Spy1  relies  on  this  pathway  to maintain pools of NSCs in he brains of NTA-Spy1 aging mice surpassing the lifespan of the NSCs in control animals. Normal brain homeostasis involves downregulation of the pools  of  active  NSCs  with  increasing  age  by  upregulation  of  CKIs  including  p16  to counteract potential oncogenic transformation 67,68 . Previously published studies showed that  aging  populations  of  NSCs  accumulated  dangerous  changes  in  critical  genes guarding  their  integrity  and  persistence  of  those  changes  results  in  tumourigenesis 68 . Spy1 can activate both CDK1 and CDK2 independently of critical phosphorylation and dephosphorylation  events  compared  to  their  cyclin-binding  partners 17 allowing  for overriding of the cell cycle checkpoints and cell cycle progression in non-permissive, cell protective circumstances. Spy1 can also promote the degradation of the CKI, p27 18 and Spy1/CDK2 complex demonstrates lower sensitivity to p21- mediated inhibition 69 . Our data demonstrate that combining downregulation of tumour suppressor levels such as p53 or PTEN and/or overexpression of potent oncogenes, frequently found in brain tumours, with upregulation of Spy1 in normal NSCs increases oncogenic transformation in vitro . NTA-Spy1 cultures  show  increased  viability  in  anchorage  independent  conditions  and enhanced  colony  formation  capacity.  We  demonstrate  that  Spy1  mediated  changes  in NSCs  drive  their  resistance  to  the  standard  chemotherapy  agent  and  the  effect  is enhanced  in  face  of  tumour  suppressor  downregulation  and/or  overexpression  of  an oncogene.  Although  further,  fine-tuned,  extensive  analysis  of  pro-oncogenic  changes cooperating  with  Spy1  to  drive  tumourigenesis in  vivo is  warranted  we  show  that downregulation of Spy1 levels in mouse glioblastoma cell line with abrogated p53 and Ras protein mutation, results in decreased tumour burden upon orthotopic injection into the syngeneic mouse model.

In summary, results of our study show that upregulation of Spy1 under the Nestin promoter  results  in  increased  proliferation  and  expansion  of  symmetrically  dividing NSCs in the adult mammalian brain, leading to decline of neurogenesis  and increased susceptibility to malignant transformation in face of selected pro-tumourigenic changes.

Limitations of the study

In  this  work  the  expression  of  Spy1  is  driven in  vivo under  the  control  of  the neuroepithelial  stem  cell  protein  (Nestin)  promoter.  The  selection  of  Nestin  promoter allows for Spy1 to be selectively driven in the neural stem cell population. This does not address the potential impact that Spy1 may have on cells outside of the neural stem cell population. Another limitation in our study is the use of lentiviral transduction of shRNA against Spy1 which is transient in nature and prevents long-term assessment of the effects of Spy1 depletion in our glioma syngeneic graft model. We also acknowledge that our study is limited to the effects mediated by one member of the Speedy/RINGO family of proteins, Spy1A. Other Spy1 family members are found to be elevated in human glioma, and  it  is  possible  that  other  family  members  play  roles  in  neural  stem  cells  and  brain development.  Our  knockdown  studies  also  do  not  address  potential  compensation  by these family members or other cell cycle proteins. The potential of redundancy becomes particularly  important when considering therapeutic translation of these findings and is worthy of future studies.

EXPERIMENTAL MODELS AND SAMPLE DETAILS

Generation of Spy1-pTRE Transgene

Site directed mutagenesis was performed to create an Xbal site in Flag-Spy1A-pLXSN 70 . The  pTRE-Tight  caspase  3  (p12):n2[TU#817]  plasmid  (Addgene  16084)  was  digested with EcoRI and Xbal to remove the caspase 3. Flag-Spy1 from the Flag-Spy1A-pLXSN vector was ligated into the pTRE-Tight backbone.

Generation and Maintenance of Spy1-pTRE Mice and Maintenance of Nestin-rtTA

Animal care and use procedures follow the Canadian Council on Animal Care Guidelines under the animal utilization protocol approved by the University of Windsor (AUPP #2209).  XhoI  enzyme  was  used  to  remove  the  Spy1-pTRE  transgene  fragment  from  the Spy1-pTRE vector.  The  transgenic  fragment  was  sent  to  London  Regional  Transgenic and  Gene  Targeting  Facility  to  use  for  pronuclear  injections  into  B6CBAF1/J  hybrid embryos. PCR analysis was used to identify founders and maintain the colony. A 25 µ L PCR reaction was prepared comprising of 100-150ng of genomic DNA, 0.4uM forward primer,  0.4uM  reverse  primer  (Supplemental  Table  1),  and  12.5 µ L  of  New  England Biolabs Master Mix. The PCR cycling conditions used are as follows: 95°C for 2 minutes and 30 seconds; 95°C for 30 seconds, 60°C for 45 seconds and 72°C for 1 minute and 30 seconds (40 cycles); this was followed by a final extension at 72°C for 10 minutes.

Nestin-rtTA mice were purchased from the Jackson Laboratory [FVB/N-Tg (NesrtTA) 306Rsv/J] or from in house breeding at the University of Windsor. Genotyping was performed using 25 µ L PCR reactions containing 1 µ L of genomic DNA, 0.4uM forward primer,  0.4uM  reverse  primer  (Supplemental  Table  1),  and  12.5 µ L  of  New  England Biolabs  Master  Mix.  The  PCR  cycling  conditions  used  are  as  follows:  94°C  for  3

minutes; 94°C for 30 seconds, 57°C for 1 minute and 72°C for 1 minute (35 cycles); this was followed by a final extension at 72°C for 2 minutes.

METHOD DETAILS

Primary Neural Stem Cell Isolations and Cell Culture

Mice were humanely euthanized, and a midline incision was made to the skin along the full length of mouse. The skull was cut at the sides and peeled off with forceps. Cranial nerves and blood vessels were cut, and forceps were used to extract the brain. The brain was transferred to a 60mm dish containing media with 3% penicillin/streptomycin (P/S) solution, sliced coronally and the area around the lateral ventricles was dissected under a microscope. Tissue was transferred to a separate dish with 3% P/S solution and, using a scalpel  blade,  tissue  was  minced  and  collected  by  centrifugation.  The  supernatant  was removed and 1 ml of Trypsin- 0.05% EDTA (Thermo) added. The cells were incubated for 1 minute at 37°C, 1 ml of media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) was added and incubated for 5 minutes and cells were collected.

Primary  cells  were  cultured  as  neurospheres  in  serum-free  Neurobasal  medium (Corning)  containing  B27  supplement  (Gibco),  hEGF  (20ng/ml;  Gibco)  and  bFGF (10ng/ml;  Sigma),  2  mM  L-glutamine  at  37°C  in  5%  CO2  in  ultra-low  cluster  plates (ULCP;  Corning). Neurospheres were subcultured every 5-10 days. Neurosphere formation assays were conducted in 6-well ULCP plates at a density of 50,000 cells per well. Clonal assays were performed in neurosphere media in 96-well ULCP plates. Cells were  plated  through  serial  dilution  and  wells  containing  1-5  cells  were  included  for experimental analysis.

For  differentiation  experiments,  single  cells  were  dissociated  from  primary neurospheres  and  seeded  in  media  supplemented  with  2%  FBS  (Gibco)  as  previously described 38 . For all lineage specific differentiation protocols, single cells were seeded in polyornithine  (Sigma)  coated plates and  supplemented  with  different  growth  mediums. For  neuronal-specific  differentiation,  cells  were  given  Neurobasal  medium  (Corning) supplemented with 2% B27 (Gibco) and 2 mM GlutaMAX-I (Gibco) for 7 days to which dibutyryl cAMP (Sigma) was added to a final concentration of 0.5mM for 3 days. For astrocyte  differentiation,  single  cells  were  seeded  in  DMEM  (Thermo)  supplemented with 1% N2 (Thermo), 2mM GlutaMAX-I (Gibco), and 1% FBS for 7 days with media changes every other day. For oligodendrocyte-specific differentiation, single cells  were seeded  in  Neurobasal  medium  (Corning)  supplemented  with  2%  B27  (Gibco),  2mM GlutaMAX-I (Gibco) and 30 ng/mL T3 (Sigma) for 7 days with media changes every other day.

Orthotopic Cell Transplantation into Mice

C57BL/6 neonatal mice at postnatal day 1 were cryoanaesthetized and transplanted with 25000 GL261 cells orthotopically into the brain using a  stereotactic injector  (Stoelting co.) coordinated using Neurostar software coordinating the injection site from bregma as the 0 point to X, Y, Z = (0.8, 2.0, -1.5mm). The cells were injected in 2ul at the rate of 30nl per min.

Quantitative Real Time-PCR (qRT-PCR) Analysis

Total  RNA  was  isolated  using  Qiagen  RNeasy  Plus  Mini  Kit  as  per  manufacturer's instructions.  cDNA  was  synthesized  using  qSCRIPT  (Quanta)  as  per  manufacturer's instructions. Reactions were prepared using Fast SYBR  Green  master mix and run/analyzed using Viia7 qRT-PCR System (Life Technologies) and software. Changes to  mRNA  expression  levels  were  presented  as  Relative  Quantification  (RQ)  values  or log10 RQ. Primer sequences are collected in supplementary material Table 1.

Bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU) Proliferation Assay

Cells were incubated in media containing BrdU (BD Pharmingen) at a concentration of 10 µM overnight at 37°C, 5% CO2. Cells were fixed in 4% PFA and incubated with 2M HCl  for 20 minutes at 37°C  and subsequently incubated with  Anti-BrdU  (BD Biosciences)  in  0.1%  Tween/PBS  for  45  minutes  at  room  temperature.  Cells  were incubated with Alexa-568 anti-mouse (Invitrogen) for 1 hour at room temperature. This was  followed  by  Hoechst  (Thermo)  counterstain  for  10  minutes  at  room  temperature. Cells  were  imaged  using  the  Leica  CTR  6500  microscope  (Leica  Microsystems, Heidelberg, Germany).

Beta-galactosidase ( β -gal) Stain

Cells were fixed in 4% PFA for 10 minutes at room temperature. Staining solution (5mM K4[Fe(CN)6]·3 H2O, 5 mM  K3[Fe(CN)6] and 2 mM MgCl2) was prepared in 1X PBS. Complete  staining  solution  was  prepared  right  before  staining  and  contained  25 µ L  of 40mg/mL X-gal per mL of staining solution. Cells were stained overnight at 37°C. Cells were imaged using the Leica CTR 6500 microscope (Leica Microsystems, Heidelberg, Germany).

Immunocytochemistry

Cells were washed with PBS and fixed in 4% PFA for 10 minutes at room temperature. Cells  were  permeabilized  using  0.2%  triton-X  in  1X  PBS  for  10  minutes  at  room temperature  and  blocked  using  10%  normal  goat  serum.  Cells  were  incubated  with primary  antibody  ( γ H2A.X;  Millipore)  for  1  hour  at  room  temperature.  Cells  were incubated  with  secondary  Alexa-fluor  anti-mouse  (Thermo)  for  45  minutes  at  room temperature. Finally, cells were counterstained with Hoechst (Thermo) for 10 minutes at room temperature. Coverslips were mounted on slides and imaged using the Leica CTR 6500 microscope (Leica Microsystems, Heidelberg, Germany).

Protein Isolation and Immunoblotting

Cells  were  lysed  with  TNE  buffer  for  20  minutes  (50mM  Tris,  150mM  NaCl,  5mM EDTA) and supplemented with protease inhibitors (2µg/mL leupeptin, 5µg/mL aprotinin, and 100µg/mL PMSF). Cells were centrifuged at 4°C at 13,000rpm for 15 minutes and the  supernatant  was  collected.  Protein  concentrations  were  determined  through  the Bradford assay as per manufacturer's instructions.

100µg of protein were loaded on a 10%- SDS polyacrylamide gel and ran for 3 hours at 120V. The gels were transferred to a PVDF membrane at 30V for 2 hours using a wet transfer method. Membranes were blocked for 1 hour at room temperature in either 2% BSA or 2% milk, depending on antibodies used. Primary antibodies were incubated overnight at 4°C. Following incubation with primary antibody, membranes were washed with tris-buffered-saline-tween 20 (TBST) 3 times for 8 minutes each. This was followed with secondary antibody incubation at room temperature for 1 hour. Following secondary antibody incubation, membranes were washed 3 times in TBST for 5-minute intervals. Protein signal was detected using chemiluminescent peroxidase substrate (Pierce) as per manufacturer's  instructions.  Images  were  taken  on  Alpha  Innotech  HD2,  densitometry and analysis were performed on AlphaEase FC software.

Antibodies

For  immunoblot  analysis,  antibodies  used  include:  Actin  (Millipore,  1:1000),  Spy1 (Abcam,  1:700),  Sox2  (Cell  Signaling,  1:1000),  Klf4  (R&D  Systems;  1:800),  p27 (Abcam, 1:800), Musashi 1 (Abcam, 1:1000), and Numb (Abcam, 1:800). All secondary antibodies, mouse IgG (Sigma Aldrich) and rabbit IgG (Sigma Aldrich), were used at a concentration of 1:10,000.

Immunofluorescent Cell Pair Assay

Single cells were isolated with 0.05% Trypsin-EDTA and plated in a drop of media onto MaxGel TM ECM (Sigma) coated coverslips. Cell division was monitored over 20 hours and  then  fixed  in  4%  PFA  for  15  minutes  at  room  temperature.  Cells  were  washed, permeabilized  and  incubated  with  1:200  Numb  antibody  (Cell  signaling).  Alexafluorophore conjugated secondary antibody (1:1000 dilution) (Invitrogen) was applied for 30 minutes. TOTO-3 was used as a nuclear counterstain. Five random fields of view were scored  for  Numb  protein  distribution  in  mitotic  cell  pairs/treatment/replicate.  Analysis was  performed  using  Leica  CTR  6500  microscope  (Leica  Microsystems,  Heidelberg, Germany) and AF software.

Novel Object Recognition Test

Novel object recognition test was performed as described 71 . Plexiglass boxes were built with the dimensions of 40cm x 40cm x 40cm and covered with frosted white paper to impair  outside  views  from  the  box.  Plastic  cubes  were  used  as  objects  and  were approximately  4cm  x  4cm  x  4cm.  The  objects  were  either  blue  or  yellow  in  colour. Computer cameras were used to record the videos. On days 1 and 2, mice were moved to an area near the boxes for 10 minutes. To acclimate the mice to their environment and decrease  stress,  the  mice  were  then  individually  placed  directly  in  the  boxes  for  10 minutes on both days. Plexiglass boxes and the objects were cleaned with 70% ethanol after every mouse and dried for 10 minutes before the next mouse was placed inside. On day 3, mice were transported to an area near boxes and allowed to sit in holding cages for 10 minutes. Similarly, mice were then placed in the boxes to explore for 10 minutes and were recorded. Day 4 was testing day, where one of the old objects was replaced with a novel object. Similarly, mice were taken to an area near the boxes, allowed to sit for 10 minutes, and then placed inside the boxes and recorded for 10 minutes. The exploration time  was  counted  by  the  period  that  the  mice  stayed  facing  the  object  within  a  5  cm radius of the object. Exploration times of under 20 seconds or over 5 minutes at an object were excluded. Preference rate was measured by dividing the total time spent exploring both objects by the time spent exploring one object.

STATISICAL ANALYSIS

Student's t -test was used and a p-value of less than 0.05 was considered significant. All data  are  reported  as  a  means  of  standard  deviation.  For  qRT-PCR  analysis,  cycle threshold  (Ct)  values  of  internal  control  gene  (GAPDH)  were  subtracted  from  the corresponding  Ct  value  of  a  target  gene  which  generated  a Δ Ct  value  which  was subjected to the Student t -test analysis. For comparative analysis between (WT), NestinrtTA, pTRE-Spy1, and NTA-Spy1mice and primary cultures, littermate mice were used.

metadata.preprint_title: Atypical Cell Cycle Regulation over Neural Stem Cell Expansion

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Atypical Cell Cycle Regulation over Neural Stem Cell Expansion
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Qemo, I.; Lubanska, D.; Stringer, K. F.; Fifield, B.-A.; Elabed, Y.; Mahendran, H. P.; Alrashed, S.; Boujeke, E.; Rodzinka, A.; Sorge, A.; Kandalam, S.; Brown, J.; Ghafoor, H.; Arshad, M.; Porter, L.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Populations of adult neural stem cells (NSCs) that reside in the mammalian brain aid in neurogenesis throughout life and can be identified by a type VI intermediate filament protein, Nestin. Cell cycle regulation plays an important role in maintaining a balance between self-renewal and differentiation and determining the fate of NSCs. Data from our group and others support that the atypical cyclin-like protein Spy1 (also called RingoA; gene SPDYA) plays a critical role in activating NSCs from a quiescent state. Elevated levels of Spy1 are found in aggressive human brain cancers, including glioblastoma. Using a conditional mouse model, we demonstrate that driving the expression of Spy1, in the Nestin-enriched NSC population of the brain, increases stemness characteristics, decreases differentiation, and increases susceptibility to oncogenic transformation. This study contributes to better understanding of intricate cell cycle mechanisms which lead to deviation from the homeostatic state, promoting aberrant changes in adult NSCs.
